where the sum runs over any fixed symmetric system of representatives of the nonzero residue classes modulo bb. The coefficients satisfy

cb,s​(0)=1b​∑m=0b−1ℓs​(m/b)=∫01ℓs​(x)​𝑑x+Of,K​(b−1),c_{b,s}(0)=\frac{1}{b}\sum_{m=0}^{b-1}\ell_{s}(m/b)=\int_{0}^{1}\ell_{s}(x)\,dx+O_{f,K}(b^{-1}),

and, for 0<|n|≤b/20<|n|\leq b/2,

cb,s​(n)=−βsb​(1−e​(−n/b)),|cb,s​(n)|≪f,K1|n|,c_{b,s}(n)=-\frac{\beta_{s}}{b\bigl(1-e(-n/b)\bigr)},\qquad|c_{b,s}(n)|\ll_{f,K}\frac{1}{|n|},

uniformly for s∈Ks\in K. Hence

∑(r,b)=1Ar​ℓs​(r¯/b)=cb,s​(0)​∑(r,b)=1Ar+∑0<|n|≤b/2cb,s​(n)​Sb​(n).\sum_{(r,b)=1}A_{r}\,\ell_{s}(\overline{r}/b)=c_{b,s}(0)\sum_{(r,b)=1}A_{r}+\sum_{0<|n|\leq b/2}c_{b,s}(n)\,S_{b}(n).

Step 4: the main term. By Lemma 22,

∑(r,b)=1Ar=φ​(b)​∫01Hs​(u)​𝑑u+OK​(bσ​τ​(b)),\sum_{(r,b)=1}A_{r}=\varphi(b)\int_{0}^{1}H_{s}(u)\,du+O_{K}\bigl(b^{\sigma}\tau(b)\bigr),

uniformly for s∈Ks\in K. Also,

G^s​(0)+cb,s​(0)=∫01Gs​(x)​𝑑x+∫01ℓs​(x)​𝑑x+Of,K​(b−1)=∫01Bs​(x)​𝑑x+Of,K​(b−1).\mkern-26.0mu\widehat{G}_{s}(0)+c_{b,s}(0)=\int_{0}^{1}G_{s}(x)\,dx+\int_{0}^{1}\ell_{s}(x)\,dx+O_{f,K}(b^{-1})=\int_{0}^{1}B_{s}(x)\,dx+O_{f,K}(b^{-1}).

Therefore the contribution of the zero Fourier mode is

φ​(b)​(∫01Hs​(u)​𝑑u)​(∫01Bs​(x)​𝑑x)+Of,K​(bσ​τ​(b))+Of,K​(1).\varphi(b)\left(\int_{0}^{1}H_{s}(u)\,du\right)\left(\int_{0}^{1}B_{s}(x)\,dx\right)+O_{f,K}\bigl(b^{\sigma}\tau(b)\bigr)+O_{f,K}(1).

Since σ<1\sigma<1, the error Of,K​(bσ​τ​(b))O_{f,K}(b^{\sigma}\tau(b)) is dominated by the final error term.

Step 5: bound the oscillatory sums Sb​(n)S_{b}(n). Let n≠0n\neq 0. Split

Sb​(n)=Sb≤R​(n)+Sb>R​(n),S_{b}(n)=S_{b}^{\leq R}(n)+S_{b}^{>R}(n),

where

Sb≤R​(n):=∑1≤r≤R(r,b)=1Ar​e​(n​r¯b),Sb>R​(n):=∑R<r≤b(r,b)=1Ar​e​(n​r¯b).S_{b}^{\leq R}(n):=\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}1\leq r\leq R\\ (r,b)=1\end{subarray}}A_{r}\,e\!\left(\frac{n\overline{r}}{b}\right),\qquad S_{b}^{>R}(n):=\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}R<r\leq b\\ (r,b)=1\end{subarray}}A_{r}\,e\!\left(\frac{n\overline{r}}{b}\right).

For the initial segment, Lemma 20 gives

|Sb≤R​(n)|≤∑r≤R|Ar|≪Kbσ​∑r≤Rr−σ≪Kbσ​R1−σ≪Kb1+σ2.|S_{b}^{\leq R}(n)|\leq\sum_{r\leq R}|A_{r}|\ll_{K}b^{\sigma}\sum_{r\leq R}r^{-\sigma}\ll_{K}b^{\sigma}R^{1-\sigma}\ll_{K}b^{\frac{1+\sigma}{2}}.

For the tail, define

ar:=𝟏(r,b)=1​e​(n​r¯b),Tn​(t):=∑1≤r≤tar.a_{r}:=\mathbf{1}_{(r,b)=1}\,e\!\left(\frac{n\overline{r}}{b}\right),\qquad T_{n}(t):=\sum_{1\leq r\leq t}a_{r}.

Then Lemma 23 gives

max1≤t≤b⁡|Tn​(t)|≪η(n,b)1/2​b1/2+η.\max_{1\leq t\leq b}|T_{n}(t)|\ll_{\eta}(n,b)^{1/2}b^{1/2+\eta}.

By Abel summation,

Sb>R​(n)=Hs​(1)​Tn​(b)−Hs​(R/b)​Tn​(R)−∑t=Rb−1Tn​(t)​(Hs​(t+1b)−Hs​(tb)).\mkern-10.0muS_{b}^{>R}(n)=H_{s}(1)\,T_{n}(b)-H_{s}(R/b)\,T_{n}(R)-\sum_{t=R}^{b-1}T_{n}(t)\Bigl(H_{s}\!\left(\frac{t+1}{b}\right)-H_{s}\!\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)\Bigr).

Using Lemma 20,

|Hs​(1)|≪K1,|Hs​(R/b)|≪K(R/b)−σ.|H_{s}(1)|\ll_{K}1,\qquad|H_{s}(R/b)|\ll_{K}(R/b)^{-\sigma}.

Also, by the mean value theorem and Lemma 21,

|Hs​(t+1b)−Hs​(tb)|≤1b​supu∈[t/b,(t+1)/b]|∂uHs​(u)|≪K1b​(tb)−σ−1≪Kbσ​t−σ−1.\mkern-22.0mu\left|H_{s}\!\left(\frac{t+1}{b}\right)-H_{s}\!\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)\right|\leq\frac{1}{b}\sup_{u\in[t/b,(t+1)/b]}|\partial_{u}H_{s}(u)|\ll_{K}\frac{1}{b}\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^{-\sigma-1}\mkern-12.0mu\ll_{K}b^{\sigma}t^{-\sigma-1}.

Hence

∑t=Rb−1|Hs​(t+1b)−Hs​(tb)|≪Kbσ​∑t≥Rt−σ−1≪Kbσ​R−σ.\sum_{t=R}^{b-1}\left|H_{s}\!\left(\frac{t+1}{b}\right)-H_{s}\!\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)\right|\ll_{K}b^{\sigma}\sum_{t\geq R}t^{-\sigma-1}\ll_{K}b^{\sigma}R^{-\sigma}.

Therefore

|Sb>R​(n)|≪K,η(n,b)1/2​b1/2+η​(1+(R/b)−σ+bσ​R−σ)≪K,η(n,b)1/2​b1+σ2+η,|S_{b}^{>R}(n)|\ll_{K,\eta}(n,b)^{1/2}b^{1/2+\eta}\Bigl(1+(R/b)^{-\sigma}+b^{\sigma}R^{-\sigma}\Bigr)\ll_{K,\eta}(n,b)^{1/2}b^{\frac{1+\sigma}{2}+\eta},

since R≍b1/2R\asymp b^{1/2}. Together with the initial-segment bound, this gives

(9) Sb​(n)≪K,η(n,b)1/2​b1+σ2+η(n≠0).S_{b}(n)\ll_{K,\eta}(n,b)^{1/2}b^{\frac{1+\sigma}{2}+\eta}\qquad(n\neq 0).

Step 6: sum the nonzero Fourier modes. For the periodic part, using (9),

∑0<|n|<N|(Gs)N^​(n)​Sb​(n)|≪f,K,ηb1+σ2+η​∑1≤n<N(n,b)1/2n.\sum_{0<|n|<N}|\widehat{(G_{s})_{N}}(n)\,S_{b}(n)|\ll_{f,K,\eta}b^{\frac{1+\sigma}{2}+\eta}\sum_{1\leq n<N}\frac{(n,b)^{1/2}}{n}.

Grouping by d=(n,b)d=(n,b), we obtain

∑1≤n<N(n,b)1/2n≤∑d∣bd−1/2​∑m<N/d1m≪τ​(b)​log⁡(2​N).\sum_{1\leq n<N}\frac{(n,b)^{1/2}}{n}\leq\sum_{d\mid b}d^{-1/2}\sum_{m<N/d}\frac{1}{m}\ll\tau(b)\log(2N).

Hence

∑0<|n|<N(Gs)N^​(n)​Sb​(n)=Of,K,η​(b1+σ2+η​τ​(b)​log⁡(2​N)).\sum_{0<|n|<N}\widehat{(G_{s})_{N}}(n)\,S_{b}(n)=O_{f,K,\eta}\!\left(b^{\frac{1+\sigma}{2}+\eta}\tau(b)\log(2N)\right).

Similarly, for the affine part,

∑0<|n|≤b/2|cb,s​(n)​Sb​(n)|≪f,K,ηb1+σ2+η​∑1≤n≤b/2(n,b)1/2n≪b1+σ2+η​τ​(b)​log⁡(2​b).\sum_{0<|n|\leq b/2}|c_{b,s}(n)\,S_{b}(n)|\ll_{f,K,\eta}b^{\frac{1+\sigma}{2}+\eta}\sum_{1\leq n\leq b/2}\frac{(n,b)^{1/2}}{n}\ll b^{\frac{1+\sigma}{2}+\eta}\tau(b)\log(2b).

Since τ​(b)​log⁡(2​b)≪ηb2​η\tau(b)\log(2b)\ll_{\eta}b^{2\eta}, both nonzero-mode contributions are

Of,K,ε​(b1+σ2+ε).O_{f,K,\varepsilon}\!\left(b^{\frac{1+\sigma}{2}+\varepsilon}\right).

The truncation error in (8) also satisfies

b1/2​log⁡(2​b)≪f,K,εb1+σ2+ε,b^{1/2}\log(2b)\ll_{f,K,\varepsilon}b^{\frac{1+\sigma}{2}+\varepsilon},

because σ>12\sigma>\tfrac{1}{2}.

Putting everything together, and recalling that

∫01Bs​(x)​𝑑x=∫01|f′′​(x)|s​𝑑x,\int_{0}^{1}B_{s}(x)\,dx=\int_{0}^{1}|f^{\prime\prime}(x)|^{s}\,dx,

we conclude that

Σb​(s)=φ​(b)​(∫01Hs​(u)​𝑑u)​(∫01|f′′​(x)|s​𝑑x)+Of,K,ε​(b1+ℜ⁡(s)2+ε),\Sigma_{b}(s)=\varphi(b)\left(\int_{0}^{1}H_{s}(u)\,du\right)\left(\int_{0}^{1}|f^{\prime\prime}(x)|^{s}\,dx\right)+O_{f,K,\varepsilon}\!\left(b^{\frac{1+\Re(s)}{2}+\varepsilon}\right),

uniformly for s∈Ks\in K. This proves the proposition. ∎

C.5. Strip-uniform refinement and half-plane continuation

In this subsection we strengthen the local continuation statement near s=23s=\tfrac{2}{3} to meromorphic continuation in the half-plane ℜ⁡(s)>35\Re(s)>\tfrac{3}{5}, and we make the dependence on the imaginary part quantitative on vertical strips. This is the analytic input needed in Appendix B, subsection B.5.

Lemma 25 (Endpoint replacement on ℜ⁡(s)>12\Re(s)>\tfrac{1}{2}).

Assume f∈C3​([0,1])f\in C^{3}([0,1]) and 0<m≤|f′′​(x)|0<m\leq|f^{\prime\prime}(x)| on [0,1][0,1]. For every compact set

K⋐{s∈ℂ:ℜ⁡(s)>12},K\Subset\{\,s\in\mathbb{C}:\Re(s)>\tfrac{1}{2}\,\},

the function

Zf​(s)−Zfend​(s)Z_{f}(s)-Z_{f}^{\mathrm{end}}(s)

is holomorphic on a neighborhood of KK. In particular, Zf−ZfendZ_{f}-Z_{f}^{\mathrm{end}} is holomorphic on the half-plane ℜ⁡(s)>12\Re(s)>\tfrac{1}{2}.

Proof.

Choose a compact set

K′⋐{s∈ℂ:ℜ⁡(s)>12}K^{\prime}\Subset\{\,s\in\mathbb{C}:\Re(s)>\tfrac{1}{2}\,\}

such that K⊂int⁡(K′)K\subset\operatorname{int}(K^{\prime}). Set

σ−:=infs∈K′ℜ⁡(s)>12.\sigma_{-}:=\inf_{s\in K^{\prime}}\Re(s)>\tfrac{1}{2}.

Recall that for s∈ℂs\in\mathbb{C} and x∈[0,1]x\in[0,1] we write

Bs​(x):=|f′′​(x)|s.B_{s}(x):=|f^{\prime\prime}(x)|^{s}.

By the regularity discussion at the beginning of this appendix, the family {Bs}s∈K′\{B_{s}\}_{s\in K^{\prime}} is uniformly C1C^{1}, so there exists CK′>0C_{K^{\prime}}>0 such that

sups∈K′‖(Bs)′‖∞≤CK′.\sup_{s\in K^{\prime}}\|(B_{s})^{\prime}\|_{\infty}\leq C_{K^{\prime}}.

Hence for all u,v∈[0,1]u,v\in[0,1] and all s∈K′s\in K^{\prime},

||f′′​(u)|s−|f′′​(v)|s|=|Bs​(u)−Bs​(v)|≤CK′​|u−v|.\bigl||f^{\prime\prime}(u)|^{s}-|f^{\prime\prime}(v)|^{s}\bigr|=|B_{s}(u)-B_{s}(v)|\leq C_{K^{\prime}}|u-v|.

Now let I=[c/d,a/b]I=[c/d,a/b] be a Farey interval. By Lemma 2,

Zf​(s)=2−s​∑I|f′′​(ξI)|s(b​d​(b+d))s,Z_{f}(s)=2^{-s}\sum_{I}\frac{|f^{\prime\prime}(\xi_{I})|^{s}}{(bd(b+d))^{s}},

while by definition

Zfend​(s)=2−s​∑I|f′′​(a/b)|s(b​d​(b+d))s.Z_{f}^{\mathrm{end}}(s)=2^{-s}\sum_{I}\frac{|f^{\prime\prime}(a/b)|^{s}}{(bd(b+d))^{s}}.

Since ξI∈(c/d,a/b)\xi_{I}\in(c/d,a/b), we have

|ξI−ab|≤|ab−cd|=1b​d.\left|\xi_{I}-\frac{a}{b}\right|\leq\left|\frac{a}{b}-\frac{c}{d}\right|=\frac{1}{bd}.

Therefore, uniformly for s∈K′s\in K^{\prime},

||f′′​(ξI)|s−|f′′​(a/b)|s(b​d​(b+d))s|≤CK′b​d​(b​d​(b+d))ℜ⁡(s)≤CK′​b−(σ−+1)​d−(σ−+1)​(b+d)−σ−.\mkern-6.0mu\left|\frac{|f^{\prime\prime}(\xi_{I})|^{s}-|f^{\prime\prime}(a/b)|^{s}}{(bd(b+d))^{s}}\right|\leq\frac{C_{K^{\prime}}}{bd\,(bd(b+d))^{\Re(s)}}\leq C_{K^{\prime}}\,b^{-(\sigma_{-}+1)}d^{-(\sigma_{-}+1)}(b+d)^{-\sigma_{-}}.

Thus the series defining Zf−ZfendZ_{f}-Z_{f}^{\mathrm{end}} is dominated on K′K^{\prime} by

∑Ib−(σ−+1)​d−(σ−+1)​(b+d)−σ−.\sum_{I}b^{-(\sigma_{-}+1)}d^{-(\sigma_{-}+1)}(b+d)^{-\sigma_{-}}.

Dropping the Farey restriction only enlarges the sum, so it is enough to consider

∑b,d≥1b−(σ−+1)​d−(σ−+1)​(b+d)−σ−.\sum_{b,d\geq 1}b^{-(\sigma_{-}+1)}d^{-(\sigma_{-}+1)}(b+d)^{-\sigma_{-}}.

Using b+d≥bb+d\geq b, we obtain

b−(σ−+1)​d−(σ−+1)​(b+d)−σ−≤b−(2​σ−+1)​d−(σ−+1).b^{-(\sigma_{-}+1)}d^{-(\sigma_{-}+1)}(b+d)^{-\sigma_{-}}\leq b^{-(2\sigma_{-}+1)}d^{-(\sigma_{-}+1)}.

Hence

∑b,d≥1b−(σ−+1)​d−(σ−+1)​(b+d)−σ−≤(∑b≥1b−(2​σ−+1))​(∑d≥1d−(σ−+1)).\sum_{b,d\geq 1}b^{-(\sigma_{-}+1)}d^{-(\sigma_{-}+1)}(b+d)^{-\sigma_{-}}\leq\left(\sum_{b\geq 1}b^{-(2\sigma_{-}+1)}\right)\left(\sum_{d\geq 1}d^{-(\sigma_{-}+1)}\right).

Both series converge because σ−>12\sigma_{-}>\tfrac{1}{2}. Therefore the defining series for Zf−ZfendZ_{f}-Z_{f}^{\mathrm{end}} converges uniformly on K′K^{\prime} by the Weierstrass MM-test. Since each term is holomorphic in ss, the sum is holomorphic on int⁡(K′)\operatorname{int}(K^{\prime}), hence on a neighborhood of KK. ∎

Lemma 26 (Strip-uniform bounds for HsH_{s} and ∂uHs\partial_{u}H_{s}).

Fix numbers

12<α<β<1.\frac{1}{2}<\alpha<\beta<1.

Then there exist constants C1=C1​(α)C_{1}=C_{1}(\alpha) and C2=C2​(α,β)C_{2}=C_{2}(\alpha,\beta) such that for every

s=σ+i​τ,α≤σ≤β,s=\sigma+i\tau,\qquad\alpha\leq\sigma\leq\beta,

and every u∈(0,1]u\in(0,1], one has

|Hs​(u)|≤C1​u−σ,|H_{s}(u)|\leq C_{1}\,u^{-\sigma},

and

|∂uHs​(u)|≤C2​(1+|τ|)​u−σ−1.|\partial_{u}H_{s}(u)|\leq C_{2}\,(1+|\tau|)\,u^{-\sigma-1}.
Proof.

Recall

Hs​(u)=∑k≥0(k+u)−s​(k+1+u)−s,ℜ⁡(s)>12,u∈(0,1].H_{s}(u)=\sum_{k\geq 0}(k+u)^{-s}(k+1+u)^{-s},\qquad\Re(s)>\frac{1}{2},\quad u\in(0,1].

We first prove the bound for Hs​(u)H_{s}(u). Split off the k=0k=0 term:

Hs​(u)=u−s​(1+u)−s+∑k≥1(k+u)−s​(k+1+u)−s.H_{s}(u)=u^{-s}(1+u)^{-s}+\sum_{k\geq 1}(k+u)^{-s}(k+1+u)^{-s}.

Since k+u>0k+u>0 and k+1+u>0k+1+u>0, we have

|(k+u)−s​(k+1+u)−s|=(k+u)−σ​(k+1+u)−σ.|(k+u)^{-s}(k+1+u)^{-s}|=(k+u)^{-\sigma}(k+1+u)^{-\sigma}.

Hence

|u−s​(1+u)−s|=u−σ​(1+u)−σ≤u−σ.|u^{-s}(1+u)^{-s}|=u^{-\sigma}(1+u)^{-\sigma}\leq u^{-\sigma}.

Also, for k≥1k\geq 1,

(k+u)−σ​(k+1+u)−σ≤k−2​σ≤k−2​α.(k+u)^{-\sigma}(k+1+u)^{-\sigma}\leq k^{-2\sigma}\leq k^{-2\alpha}.

Therefore

∑k≥1(k+u)−σ(k+1+u)−σ≤∑k≥1k−2​α=:Cα<∞.\sum_{k\geq 1}(k+u)^{-\sigma}(k+1+u)^{-\sigma}\leq\sum_{k\geq 1}k^{-2\alpha}=:C_{\alpha}<\infty.

Since u∈(0,1]u\in(0,1], we have u−σ≥1u^{-\sigma}\geq 1, so

Cα≤Cα​u−σ.C_{\alpha}\leq C_{\alpha}\,u^{-\sigma}.

Combining the two estimates gives

|Hs​(u)|≤(1+Cα)​u−σ,|H_{s}(u)|\leq(1+C_{\alpha})\,u^{-\sigma},

which proves the first bound.

We now turn to the derivative. On every interval u∈[δ,1]u\in[\delta,1] with δ>0\delta>0, the defining series and the differentiated series converge uniformly, so termwise differentiation is justified. We obtain

∂uHs​(u)=−s​∑k≥0(k+u)−s−1​(k+1+u)−s−s​∑k≥0(k+u)−s​(k+1+u)−s−1.\partial_{u}H_{s}(u)=-s\sum_{k\geq 0}(k+u)^{-s-1}(k+1+u)^{-s}-s\sum_{k\geq 0}(k+u)^{-s}(k+1+u)^{-s-1}.

For the k=0k=0 term, we have

|s|​(u−σ−1​(1+u)−σ+u−σ​(1+u)−σ−1)≤2​|s|​u−σ−1.|s|\Bigl(u^{-\sigma-1}(1+u)^{-\sigma}+u^{-\sigma}(1+u)^{-\sigma-1}\Bigr)\leq 2|s|\,u^{-\sigma-1}.

For the tail k≥1k\geq 1, each summand is bounded by

|s|​(k+u)−σ−1​(k+1+u)−σ+|s|​(k+u)−σ​(k+1+u)−σ−1≤2​|s|​k−2​σ−1≤2​|s|​k−2​α−1.\mkern-22.0mu|s|\,(k+u)^{-\sigma-1}(k+1+u)^{-\sigma}+|s|\,(k+u)^{-\sigma}(k+1+u)^{-\sigma-1}\leq 2|s|\,k^{-2\sigma-1}\leq 2|s|\,k^{-2\alpha-1}.

Since ∑k≥1k−2​α−1<∞\sum_{k\geq 1}k^{-2\alpha-1}<\infty, the full tail is bounded by Cα′​|s|C^{\prime}_{\alpha}|s|, and again u−σ−1≥1u^{-\sigma-1}\geq 1 on (0,1](0,1]. Therefore

|∂uHs​(u)|≤Cα′′​|s|​u−σ−1.|\partial_{u}H_{s}(u)|\leq C^{\prime\prime}_{\alpha}|s|\,u^{-\sigma-1}.

On the strip α≤σ≤β\alpha\leq\sigma\leq\beta,

|s|=|σ+i​τ|≤β+|τ|≤(1+β)​(1+|τ|),|s|=|\sigma+i\tau|\leq\beta+|\tau|\leq(1+\beta)(1+|\tau|),

so

|∂uHs​(u)|≤C2​(α,β)​(1+|τ|)​u−σ−1.|\partial_{u}H_{s}(u)|\leq C_{2}(\alpha,\beta)\,(1+|\tau|)\,u^{-\sigma-1}.

∎

Lemma 27 (Strip-uniform bounds for Bs​(x)=|f′′​(x)|sB_{s}(x)=|f^{\prime\prime}(x)|^{s}).

Fix numbers

12<α<β<1.\frac{1}{2}<\alpha<\beta<1.

Assume

f∈C3​([0,1]),0<m≤|f′′​(x)|≤M(x∈[0,1]).f\in C^{3}([0,1]),\qquad 0<m\leq|f^{\prime\prime}(x)|\leq M\quad(x\in[0,1]).

Then there exist constants C0,C1,C2>0C_{0},C_{1},C_{2}>0, depending only on α,β,f\alpha,\beta,f, such that for all

s=σ+i​τ,α≤σ≤β,s=\sigma+i\tau,\qquad\alpha\leq\sigma\leq\beta,

and all x∈[0,1]x\in[0,1], one has

|Bs​(x)|≤C0,‖Bs‖∞≤C1,‖Bs′‖∞≤C2​(1+|τ|).|B_{s}(x)|\leq C_{0},\qquad\|B_{s}\|_{\infty}\leq C_{1},\qquad\|B_{s}^{\prime}\|_{\infty}\leq C_{2}(1+|\tau|).

In particular, the affine interpolant/remainder decomposition used in the proof of Proposition 2 remains valid on strips, with polynomial dependence on |τ||\tau|.

Proof.

Since 0<m≤|f′′​(x)|≤M0<m\leq|f^{\prime\prime}(x)|\leq M on [0,1][0,1], for s=σ+i​τs=\sigma+i\tau and x∈[0,1]x\in[0,1],

|Bs​(x)|=||f′′​(x)|s|=|f′′​(x)|σ≤Mβ.|B_{s}(x)|=\bigl||f^{\prime\prime}(x)|^{s}\bigr|=|f^{\prime\prime}(x)|^{\sigma}\leq M^{\beta}.

Thus we may take

C0=C1=Mβ.C_{0}=C_{1}=M^{\beta}.

Now differentiate. Since f′′f^{\prime\prime} has constant sign on [0,1][0,1] and is bounded away from 0, the function log⁡|f′′​(x)|\log|f^{\prime\prime}(x)| is C1C^{1}, and

Bs′​(x)=s​|f′′​(x)|s​f′′′​(x)f′′​(x)​sgn⁡(f′′​(x)).B_{s}^{\prime}(x)=s\,|f^{\prime\prime}(x)|^{s}\,\frac{f^{\prime\prime\prime}(x)}{f^{\prime\prime}(x)}\,\operatorname{sgn}(f^{\prime\prime}(x)).

Therefore

|Bs′​(x)|≤|s|​|f′′​(x)|σ​|f′′′​(x)f′′​(x)|≤|s|​Mβ​‖f′′′‖∞m.|B_{s}^{\prime}(x)|\leq|s|\,|f^{\prime\prime}(x)|^{\sigma}\left|\frac{f^{\prime\prime\prime}(x)}{f^{\prime\prime}(x)}\right|\leq|s|\,M^{\beta}\,\frac{\|f^{\prime\prime\prime}\|_{\infty}}{m}.

On the strip α≤σ≤β\alpha\leq\sigma\leq\beta,

|s|=|σ+i​τ|≤β+|τ|≤(1+β)​(1+|τ|),|s|=|\sigma+i\tau|\leq\beta+|\tau|\leq(1+\beta)(1+|\tau|),

so

‖Bs′‖∞≤(1+β)​Mβ​‖f′′′‖∞m​(1+|τ|).\|B_{s}^{\prime}\|_{\infty}\leq(1+\beta)\,M^{\beta}\,\frac{\|f^{\prime\prime\prime}\|_{\infty}}{m}\,(1+|\tau|).

Finally, in the proof of Proposition 2, one writes

ℓs​(x):=(1−x)​Bs​(0)+x​Bs​(1),Gs​(x):=Bs​(x)−ℓs​(x).\ell_{s}(x):=(1-x)B_{s}(0)+xB_{s}(1),\qquad G_{s}(x):=B_{s}(x)-\ell_{s}(x).

The bounds above imply

|Bs​(1)−Bs​(0)|≤2​‖Bs‖∞≪α,β,f1,|B_{s}(1)-B_{s}(0)|\leq 2\|B_{s}\|_{\infty}\ll_{\alpha,\beta,f}1,

hence

Lip⁡(ℓs)≪α,β,f1,\operatorname{Lip}(\ell_{s})\ll_{\alpha,\beta,f}1,

while

Lip⁡(Gs)≤‖Bs′‖∞+Lip⁡(ℓs)≪α,β,f(1+|τ|).\operatorname{Lip}(G_{s})\leq\|B_{s}^{\prime}\|_{\infty}+\operatorname{Lip}(\ell_{s})\ll_{\alpha,\beta,f}(1+|\tau|).

∎

Proposition 11 (Strip-uniform equidistribution estimate).

Fix numbers

12<α<β<1.\frac{1}{2}<\alpha<\beta<1.

Assume

f∈C3​([0,1]),0<m≤|f′′​(x)|≤M(x∈[0,1]).f\in C^{3}([0,1]),\qquad 0<m\leq|f^{\prime\prime}(x)|\leq M\quad(x\in[0,1]).

Then for every ε>0\varepsilon>0 there exists C=C​(α,β,f,ε)>0C=C(\alpha,\beta,f,\varepsilon)>0 such that for every

s=σ+i​τ,α≤σ≤β,s=\sigma+i\tau,\qquad\alpha\leq\sigma\leq\beta,

and every b≥1b\geq 1, one has

Σb​(s)=φ​(b)​(∫01Hs​(u)​𝑑u)​(∫01|f′′​(v)|s​𝑑v)+O​((1+|τ|)2​b1+σ2+ε),\Sigma_{b}(s)=\varphi(b)\left(\int_{0}^{1}H_{s}(u)\,du\right)\left(\int_{0}^{1}|f^{\prime\prime}(v)|^{s}\,dv\right)+O\!\left((1+|\tau|)^{2}\,b^{\frac{1+\sigma}{2}+\varepsilon}\right),

where

Σb​(s):=∑1≤r≤b(r,b)=1Hs​(r/b)​|f′′​(r¯/b)|s.\Sigma_{b}(s):=\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}1\leq r\leq b\\ (r,b)=1\end{subarray}}H_{s}(r/b)\,|f^{\prime\prime}(\overline{r}/b)|^{s}.
Proof.

Write

Bs​(x):=|f′′​(x)|s,Ar:=Hs​(r/b).B_{s}(x):=|f^{\prime\prime}(x)|^{s},\qquad A_{r}:=H_{s}(r/b).

We first record the strip-uniform analogue of Lemma 22:

(10) ∑1≤r≤b(r,b)=1Hs​(r/b)=φ​(b)​∫01Hs​(u)​𝑑u+Oα,β​((1+|τ|)​bσ​τ​(b)).\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}1\leq r\leq b\\ (r,b)=1\end{subarray}}H_{s}(r/b)=\varphi(b)\int_{0}^{1}H_{s}(u)\,du+O_{\alpha,\beta}\!\left((1+|\tau|)\,b^{\sigma}\tau(b)\right).

Indeed, the proof of Lemma 22 carries over verbatim once one replaces the compact-uniform bounds used there by the strip-uniform bounds of Lemma 26. The only place where |τ||\tau| appears is through the derivative bound for HsH_{s}, and this yields the factor (1+|τ|)(1+|\tau|).

Now follow the proof of Proposition 2. Let

ℓs​(x):=(1−x)​Bs​(0)+x​Bs​(1),Gs​(x):=Bs​(x)−ℓs​(x).\ell_{s}(x):=(1-x)B_{s}(0)+xB_{s}(1),\qquad G_{s}(x):=B_{s}(x)-\ell_{s}(x).

By Lemma 27,

‖Bs‖∞≪α,β,f1,Lip⁡(Gs)≪α,β,f(1+|τ|).\|B_{s}\|_{\infty}\ll_{\alpha,\beta,f}1,\qquad\operatorname{Lip}(G_{s})\ll_{\alpha,\beta,f}(1+|\tau|).

Set

N:=R:=⌊b1/2⌋.N:=R:=\lfloor b^{1/2}\rfloor.

By the Fejér-kernel estimate (Lemma 24),

‖Gs−(Gs)N‖∞≪log⁡(2​N)N​Lip⁡(Gs)≪α,β,f(1+|τ|)​log⁡(2​N)N.\|G_{s}-(G_{s})_{N}\|_{\infty}\ll\frac{\log(2N)}{N}\operatorname{Lip}(G_{s})\ll_{\alpha,\beta,f}(1+|\tau|)\frac{\log(2N)}{N}.

Therefore

Σb​(s)=∑(r,b)=1Ar​(Gs)N​(r¯/b)+∑(r,b)=1Ar​ℓs​(r¯/b)+O​((1+|τ|)​log⁡(2​N)N​∑(r,b)=1|Ar|).\mkern-25.0mu\Sigma_{b}(s)=\sum_{(r,b)=1}A_{r}\,(G_{s})_{N}(\overline{r}/b)+\sum_{(r,b)=1}A_{r}\,\ell_{s}(\overline{r}/b)+O\!\left((1+|\tau|)\frac{\log(2N)}{N}\sum_{(r,b)=1}|A_{r}|\right).

By Lemma 26,

|Ar|=|Hs​(r/b)|≪α(r/b)−σ,|A_{r}|=|H_{s}(r/b)|\ll_{\alpha}(r/b)^{-\sigma},

so

∑(r,b)=1|Ar|≤∑r=1b|Hs​(r/b)|≪αbσ​∑r=1br−σ≪αb.\sum_{(r,b)=1}|A_{r}|\leq\sum_{r=1}^{b}|H_{s}(r/b)|\ll_{\alpha}b^{\sigma}\sum_{r=1}^{b}r^{-\sigma}\ll_{\alpha}b.

Hence the truncation error is

(11) O​((1+|τ|)​b1/2​log⁡(2​b)).O\!\left((1+|\tau|)\,b^{1/2}\log(2b)\right).

Next expand the periodic part:

(Gs)N​(x)=G^s​(0)+∑0<|n|<N(Gs)N^​(n)​e​(n​x).(G_{s})_{N}(x)=\widehat{G}_{s}(0)+\sum_{0<|n|<N}\widehat{(G_{s})_{N}}(n)e(nx).

Since GsG_{s} is Lipschitz with

Lip⁡(Gs)≪(1+|τ|),\operatorname{Lip}(G_{s})\ll(1+|\tau|),

its Fourier coefficients satisfy

|G^s​(n)|≪α,β,f1+|τ||n|(n≠0),|\widehat{G}_{s}(n)|\ll_{\alpha,\beta,f}\frac{1+|\tau|}{|n|}\qquad(n\neq 0),

hence

|(Gs)N^​(n)|≤|G^s​(n)|≪α,β,f1+|τ||n|.|\widehat{(G_{s})_{N}}(n)|\leq|\widehat{G}_{s}(n)|\ll_{\alpha,\beta,f}\frac{1+|\tau|}{|n|}.

So

∑(r,b)=1Ar​(Gs)N​(r¯/b)=G^s​(0)​∑(r,b)=1Ar+∑0<|n|<N(Gs)N^​(n)​Sb​(n),\sum_{(r,b)=1}A_{r}\,(G_{s})_{N}(\overline{r}/b)=\widehat{G}_{s}(0)\sum_{(r,b)=1}A_{r}+\sum_{0<|n|<N}\widehat{(G_{s})_{N}}(n)\,S_{b}(n),

where

Sb​(n):=∑(r,b)=1Ar​e​(n​r¯b).S_{b}(n):=\sum_{(r,b)=1}A_{r}\,e\!\left(\frac{n\overline{r}}{b}\right).

For the affine part, write

ℓs​(x)=αs+βs​x,βs=Bs​(1)−Bs​(0).\ell_{s}(x)=\alpha_{s}+\beta_{s}x,\qquad\beta_{s}=B_{s}(1)-B_{s}(0).

Since ‖Bs‖∞≪1\|B_{s}\|_{\infty}\ll 1, we have |βs|≪1|\beta_{s}|\ll 1. The discrete Fourier expansion on the bb-grid gives

ℓs​(m/b)=cb,s​(0)+∑0<|n|≤b/2cb,s​(n)​e​(n​m/b),\ell_{s}(m/b)=c_{b,s}(0)+\sum_{0<|n|\leq b/2}c_{b,s}(n)e(nm/b),

with

cb,s​(0)=∫01ℓs​(x)​𝑑x+O​(b−1),|cb,s​(n)|≪α,β,f1|n|(0<|n|≤b/2).c_{b,s}(0)=\int_{0}^{1}\ell_{s}(x)\,dx+O(b^{-1}),\qquad|c_{b,s}(n)|\ll_{\alpha,\beta,f}\frac{1}{|n|}\quad(0<|n|\leq b/2).

Therefore

∑(r,b)=1Ar​ℓs​(r¯/b)=cb,s​(0)​∑(r,b)=1Ar+∑0<|n|≤b/2cb,s​(n)​Sb​(n).\sum_{(r,b)=1}A_{r}\,\ell_{s}(\overline{r}/b)=c_{b,s}(0)\sum_{(r,b)=1}A_{r}+\sum_{0<|n|\leq b/2}c_{b,s}(n)S_{b}(n).

We now bound Sb​(n)S_{b}(n). Split

Sb​(n)=Sb≤R​(n)+Sb>R​(n),S_{b}(n)=S_{b}^{\leq R}(n)+S_{b}^{>R}(n),

where

Sb≤R​(n):=∑1≤r≤R(r,b)=1Ar​e​(n​r¯b),Sb>R​(n):=∑R<r≤b(r,b)=1Ar​e​(n​r¯b).S_{b}^{\leq R}(n):=\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}1\leq r\leq R\\ (r,b)=1\end{subarray}}A_{r}e\!\left(\frac{n\overline{r}}{b}\right),\qquad S_{b}^{>R}(n):=\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}R<r\leq b\\ (r,b)=1\end{subarray}}A_{r}e\!\left(\frac{n\overline{r}}{b}\right).

For the initial segment,

|Sb≤R​(n)|≤∑r≤R|Ar|≪αbσ​∑r≤Rr−σ≪αbσ​R1−σ≪αb1+σ2.|S_{b}^{\leq R}(n)|\leq\sum_{r\leq R}|A_{r}|\ll_{\alpha}b^{\sigma}\sum_{r\leq R}r^{-\sigma}\ll_{\alpha}b^{\sigma}R^{1-\sigma}\ll_{\alpha}b^{\frac{1+\sigma}{2}}.

For the tail, define

ar:=𝟏(r,b)=1​e​(n​r¯b),Tn​(T):=∑1≤r≤Tar.a_{r}:=\mathbf{1}_{(r,b)=1}e\!\left(\frac{n\overline{r}}{b}\right),\qquad T_{n}(T):=\sum_{1\leq r\leq T}a_{r}.

By Lemma 23,

max1≤T≤b⁡|Tn​(T)|≪η(n,b)1/2​b1/2+η\max_{1\leq T\leq b}|T_{n}(T)|\ll_{\eta}(n,b)^{1/2}b^{1/2+\eta}

for any η>0\eta>0. Abel summation gives

Sb>R​(n)=Hs​(1)​Tn​(b)−Hs​(R/b)​Tn​(R)−∑T=Rb−1Tn​(T)​(Hs​((T+1)/b)−Hs​(T/b)).S_{b}^{>R}(n)=H_{s}(1)\,T_{n}(b)-H_{s}(R/b)\,T_{n}(R)-\sum_{T=R}^{b-1}T_{n}(T)\Bigl(H_{s}((T+1)/b)-H_{s}(T/b)\Bigr).

By Lemma 26,

|Hs​(1)|≪α1,|Hs​(R/b)|≪α(R/b)−σ.|H_{s}(1)|\ll_{\alpha}1,\qquad|H_{s}(R/b)|\ll_{\alpha}(R/b)^{-\sigma}.

Also, by the mean value theorem and Lemma 26,

|Hs​((T+1)/b)−Hs​(T/b)|≤1b​supu∈[T/b,(T+1)/b]|∂uHs​(u)|≪α,β(1+|τ|)​bσ​T−σ−1.|H_{s}((T+1)/b)-H_{s}(T/b)|\leq\frac{1}{b}\sup_{u\in[T/b,(T+1)/b]}|\partial_{u}H_{s}(u)|\ll_{\alpha,\beta}(1+|\tau|)\,b^{\sigma}T^{-\sigma-1}.

Hence

∑T=Rb−1|Hs​((T+1)/b)−Hs​(T/b)|≪α,β(1+|τ|)​bσ​∑T≥RT−σ−1≪α,β(1+|τ|)​bσ​R−σ.\sum_{T=R}^{b-1}|H_{s}((T+1)/b)-H_{s}(T/b)|\ll_{\alpha,\beta}(1+|\tau|)\,b^{\sigma}\sum_{T\geq R}T^{-\sigma-1}\ll_{\alpha,\beta}(1+|\tau|)\,b^{\sigma}R^{-\sigma}.

Therefore

|Sb>R​(n)|≪α,β,η(n,b)1/2​b1/2+η​(1+(R/b)−σ+(1+|τ|)​bσ​R−σ).|S_{b}^{>R}(n)|\ll_{\alpha,\beta,\eta}(n,b)^{1/2}b^{1/2+\eta}\left(1+(R/b)^{-\sigma}+(1+|\tau|)b^{\sigma}R^{-\sigma}\right).

Since R=⌊b1/2⌋R=\lfloor b^{1/2}\rfloor, both (R/b)−σ(R/b)^{-\sigma} and bσ​R−σb^{\sigma}R^{-\sigma} are ≪bσ/2\ll b^{\sigma/2}. Thus

Sb​(n)≪α,β,η(1+|τ|)​(n,b)1/2​b1+σ2+η.S_{b}(n)\ll_{\alpha,\beta,\eta}(1+|\tau|)\,(n,b)^{1/2}\,b^{\frac{1+\sigma}{2}+\eta}.

Now sum the nonzero Fourier modes. For the periodic part,

∑0<|n|<N|(Gs)N^​(n)​Sb​(n)|≪α,β,f,η(1+|τ|)2​b1+σ2+η​∑1≤n<N(n,b)1/2n.\sum_{0<|n|<N}|\widehat{(G_{s})_{N}}(n)S_{b}(n)|\ll_{\alpha,\beta,f,\eta}(1+|\tau|)^{2}b^{\frac{1+\sigma}{2}+\eta}\sum_{1\leq n<N}\frac{(n,b)^{1/2}}{n}.

Grouping by d=(n,b)d=(n,b) exactly as in the proof of Proposition 2,

∑1≤n<N(n,b)1/2n≪τ​(b)​log⁡(2​N).\sum_{1\leq n<N}\frac{(n,b)^{1/2}}{n}\ll\tau(b)\log(2N).

Therefore

∑0<|n|<N(Gs)N^​(n)​Sb​(n)=O​((1+|τ|)2​b1+σ2+η​τ​(b)​log⁡(2​N)).\sum_{0<|n|<N}\widehat{(G_{s})_{N}}(n)S_{b}(n)=O\!\left((1+|\tau|)^{2}b^{\frac{1+\sigma}{2}+\eta}\tau(b)\log(2N)\right).

Similarly, for the affine part,

∑0<|n|≤b/2|cb,s​(n)​Sb​(n)|≪α,β,f,η(1+|τ|)​b1+σ2+η​∑1≤n≤b/2(n,b)1/2n,\sum_{0<|n|\leq b/2}|c_{b,s}(n)S_{b}(n)|\ll_{\alpha,\beta,f,\eta}(1+|\tau|)\,b^{\frac{1+\sigma}{2}+\eta}\sum_{1\leq n\leq b/2}\frac{(n,b)^{1/2}}{n},

hence

∑0<|n|≤b/2cb,s​(n)​Sb​(n)=O​((1+|τ|)​b1+σ2+η​τ​(b)​log⁡(2​b)).\sum_{0<|n|\leq b/2}c_{b,s}(n)S_{b}(n)=O\!\left((1+|\tau|)\,b^{\frac{1+\sigma}{2}+\eta}\tau(b)\log(2b)\right).

For the zero mode,

G^s​(0)+cb,s​(0)=∫01Bs​(x)​𝑑x+O​(b−1)=∫01|f′′​(x)|s​𝑑x+O​(b−1),\widehat{G}_{s}(0)+c_{b,s}(0)=\int_{0}^{1}B_{s}(x)\,dx+O(b^{-1})=\int_{0}^{1}|f^{\prime\prime}(x)|^{s}\,dx+O(b^{-1}),

so by (10),

(G^s​(0)+cb,s​(0))​∑(r,b)=1Ar=\bigl(\widehat{G}_{s}(0)+c_{b,s}(0)\bigr)\sum_{(r,b)=1}A_{r}=
φ​(b)​(∫01Hs​(u)​𝑑u)​(∫01|f′′​(x)|s​𝑑x)+O​((1+|τ|)​bσ​τ​(b))+O​(1).\varphi(b)\left(\int_{0}^{1}H_{s}(u)\,du\right)\left(\int_{0}^{1}|f^{\prime\prime}(x)|^{s}\,dx\right)+O\!\left((1+|\tau|)\,b^{\sigma}\tau(b)\right)+O(1).

Since σ<1\sigma<1, we have

bσ​τ​(b)≪εb1+σ2+ε,b^{\sigma}\tau(b)\ll_{\varepsilon}b^{\frac{1+\sigma}{2}+\varepsilon},

and because σ>12\sigma>\tfrac{1}{2},

b1/2​log⁡(2​b)≪εb1+σ2+ε.b^{1/2}\log(2b)\ll_{\varepsilon}b^{\frac{1+\sigma}{2}+\varepsilon}.

Absorbing the factors τ​(b)​log⁡(2​b)\tau(b)\log(2b) into bεb^{\varepsilon}, and then choosing η>0\eta>0 sufficiently small relative to the final ε\varepsilon, all the error terms combine into

O​((1+|τ|)2​b1+σ2+ε).O\!\left((1+|\tau|)^{2}\,b^{\frac{1+\sigma}{2}+\varepsilon}\right).

This proves the proposition. ∎

Proposition 12 (Strip-uniform holomorphy and growth of the error term).

Fix numbers

35<α<β<1.\frac{3}{5}<\alpha<\beta<1.

Define

A​(s):=2−s​(∫01Hs​(u)​𝑑u)​(∫01|f′′​(v)|s​𝑑v)A(s):=2^{-s}\left(\int_{0}^{1}H_{s}(u)\,du\right)\left(\int_{0}^{1}|f^{\prime\prime}(v)|^{s}\,dv\right)

and

E​(s):=2−s​∑b≥1b−3​s​Rb​(s),E(s):=2^{-s}\sum_{b\geq 1}b^{-3s}R_{b}(s),

where

Σb​(s)=φ​(b)​(∫01Hs​(u)​𝑑u)​(∫01|f′′​(v)|s​𝑑v)+Rb​(s).\Sigma_{b}(s)=\varphi(b)\left(\int_{0}^{1}H_{s}(u)\,du\right)\left(\int_{0}^{1}|f^{\prime\prime}(v)|^{s}\,dv\right)+R_{b}(s).

Then E​(s)E(s) is holomorphic on the strip

α<ℜ⁡(s)<β,\alpha<\Re(s)<\beta,

and

E​(σ+i​τ)≪α,β,f(1+|τ|)2(α≤σ≤β).E(\sigma+i\tau)\ll_{\alpha,\beta,f}(1+|\tau|)^{2}\qquad(\alpha\leq\sigma\leq\beta).

Consequently,

Zfend​(s)=A​(s)​∑b≥1φ​(b)b3​s+E​(s)Z_{f}^{\mathrm{end}}(s)=A(s)\sum_{b\geq 1}\frac{\varphi(b)}{b^{3s}}+E(s)

holds on the strip, with EE holomorphic and of polynomial growth on vertical lines.

Proof.

Let

s=σ+i​τ,α≤σ≤β.s=\sigma+i\tau,\qquad\alpha\leq\sigma\leq\beta.

By Proposition 11,

|Rb​(s)|≪α,β,f,ε(1+|τ|)2​b1+σ2+ε.|R_{b}(s)|\ll_{\alpha,\beta,f,\varepsilon}(1+|\tau|)^{2}\,b^{\frac{1+\sigma}{2}+\varepsilon}.

Therefore

|b−3​s​Rb​(s)|=b−3​σ​|Rb​(s)|≪α,β,f,ε(1+|τ|)2​b−3​σ+1+σ2+ε.|b^{-3s}R_{b}(s)|=b^{-3\sigma}|R_{b}(s)|\ll_{\alpha,\beta,f,\varepsilon}(1+|\tau|)^{2}\,b^{-3\sigma+\frac{1+\sigma}{2}+\varepsilon}.

The exponent simplifies to

−3​σ+1+σ2+ε=−5​σ−12+ε.-3\sigma+\frac{1+\sigma}{2}+\varepsilon=-\frac{5\sigma-1}{2}+\varepsilon.

Since σ≥α\sigma\geq\alpha, we obtain the uniform bound

|b−3​s​Rb​(s)|≪α,β,f,ε(1+|τ|)2​b−5​α−12+ε.|b^{-3s}R_{b}(s)|\ll_{\alpha,\beta,f,\varepsilon}(1+|\tau|)^{2}\,b^{-\frac{5\alpha-1}{2}+\varepsilon}.

Now α>35\alpha>\tfrac{3}{5}, so

5​α−12>1.\frac{5\alpha-1}{2}>1.

Choose ε>0\varepsilon>0 so small that

−5​α−12+ε<−1.-\frac{5\alpha-1}{2}+\varepsilon<-1.

Then

∑b≥1b−5​α−12+ε\sum_{b\geq 1}b^{-\frac{5\alpha-1}{2}+\varepsilon}

converges. Hence, for every fixed τ\tau,

∑b≥1b−3​s​Rb​(s)\sum_{b\geq 1}b^{-3s}R_{b}(s)

converges absolutely and uniformly in σ∈[α,β]\sigma\in[\alpha,\beta], with the bound

∑b≥1|b−3​s​Rb​(s)|≪α,β,f(1+|τ|)2.\sum_{b\geq 1}|b^{-3s}R_{b}(s)|\ll_{\alpha,\beta,f}(1+|\tau|)^{2}.

Since

|2−s|=2−σ≤2−α,|2^{-s}|=2^{-\sigma}\leq 2^{-\alpha},

the same bound holds for the full error term:

E​(σ+i​τ)≪α,β,f(1+|τ|)2.E(\sigma+i\tau)\ll_{\alpha,\beta,f}(1+|\tau|)^{2}.

This proves the strip-uniform growth estimate.

To prove holomorphy, let KK be any compact subset of the open strip

α<ℜ⁡(s)<β.\alpha<\Re(s)<\beta.

Then there exist α′,β′\alpha^{\prime},\beta^{\prime} with

α<α′<ℜ⁡(s)<β′<β(s∈K).\alpha<\alpha^{\prime}<\Re(s)<\beta^{\prime}<\beta\qquad(s\in K).

Repeating the same argument with α′\alpha^{\prime} in place of α\alpha, we obtain a convergent numerical majorant independent of s∈Ks\in K. Hence the series defining E​(s)E(s) converges uniformly on KK by the Weierstrass MM-test. Since each summand is holomorphic in ss, it follows that E​(s)E(s) is holomorphic on the open strip α<ℜ⁡(s)<β\alpha<\Re(s)<\beta.

Finally, by construction,

Zfend​(s)=A​(s)​∑b≥1φ​(b)b3​s+E​(s)Z_{f}^{\mathrm{end}}(s)=A(s)\sum_{b\geq 1}\frac{\varphi(b)}{b^{3s}}+E(s)

holds on the strip. Here A​(s)A(s) is holomorphic on 12<ℜ⁡(s)<1\tfrac{1}{2}<\Re(s)<1 by Lemma 5 and dominated convergence. ∎

Theorem 9 (Half-plane refinement of Theorem 3).

Assume (3). Then the local meromorphic continuation from Theorem 3 extends to the half-plane

ℜ⁡(s)>35.\Re(s)>\frac{3}{5}.

It is holomorphic there except for a simple pole at

s=23.s=\frac{2}{3}.

Moreover,

Ress=2/3Zf​(s)=3​Γ​(1/3)322/3​π3​∫01|f′′​(v)|2/3​𝑑v.\operatorname*{Res}_{s=2/3}Z_{f}(s)=\frac{\sqrt{3}\,\Gamma(1/3)^{3}}{2^{2/3}\pi^{3}}\int_{0}^{1}|f^{\prime\prime}(v)|^{2/3}\,dv.
Proof.

First consider a point s0s_{0} with ℜ⁡(s0)>35\Re(s_{0})>\tfrac{3}{5} and s0≠23s_{0}\neq\tfrac{2}{3}. Choose

35<α<ℜ⁡(s0)<β<1\frac{3}{5}<\alpha<\Re(s_{0})<\beta<1

such that s0∈{α<ℜ⁡(s)<β}s_{0}\in\{\alpha<\Re(s)<\beta\} and 2/3∈(α,β)2/3\in(\alpha,\beta). By Proposition 12,

Zfend​(s)=A​(s)​∑b≥1φ​(b)b3​s+E​(s)=A​(s)​ζ​(3​s−1)ζ​(3​s)+E​(s)Z_{f}^{\mathrm{end}}(s)=A(s)\sum_{b\geq 1}\frac{\varphi(b)}{b^{3s}}+E(s)=A(s)\frac{\zeta(3s-1)}{\zeta(3s)}+E(s)

in a neighborhood of s0s_{0}, where EE is holomorphic there. Since ℜ⁡(s)>35\Re(s)>\tfrac{3}{5} implies ℜ⁡(3​s)>95>1\Re(3s)>\tfrac{9}{5}>1, we have ζ​(3​s)≠0\zeta(3s)\neq 0 in this half-plane. Therefore the only possible singularity of the main term is at s=23s=\tfrac{2}{3}. Thus ZfendZ_{f}^{\mathrm{end}}, and hence ZfZ_{f} by Lemma 25, is holomorphic near every s0≠23s_{0}\neq\tfrac{2}{3} with ℜ⁡(s0)>35\Re(s_{0})>\tfrac{3}{5}.

Now consider s0=23s_{0}=\tfrac{2}{3}. Choose any strip

35<α<23<β<1.\frac{3}{5}<\alpha<\frac{2}{3}<\beta<1.

The same decomposition holds there, and again ζ​(3​s)≠0\zeta(3s)\neq 0. Since

ζ​(3​s−1)ζ​(3​s)\frac{\zeta(3s-1)}{\zeta(3s)}

has a simple pole at s=23s=\tfrac{2}{3}, it follows that Zfend​(s)Z_{f}^{\mathrm{end}}(s), and hence Zf​(s)Z_{f}(s), has a simple pole there.

These local meromorphic continuations agree on overlaps, because each of them coincides with the original absolutely convergent Dirichlet series on the nonempty open set where ℜ⁡(s)>23\Re(s)>\tfrac{2}{3}. Hence they glue uniquely to a meromorphic continuation of Zf​(s)Z_{f}(s) to the whole half-plane ℜ⁡(s)>35\Re(s)>\tfrac{3}{5}.

The residue computation is the same as in the current proof of Theorem 3. Namely,

Ress=2/3Zf​(s)=A​(2/3)⋅13​ζ​(2).\operatorname*{Res}_{s=2/3}Z_{f}(s)=A(2/3)\cdot\frac{1}{3\zeta(2)}.

Now

A​(2/3)=2−2/3​(∫01H2/3​(u)​𝑑u)​(∫01|f′′​(v)|2/3​𝑑v),A(2/3)=2^{-2/3}\left(\int_{0}^{1}H_{2/3}(u)\,du\right)\left(\int_{0}^{1}|f^{\prime\prime}(v)|^{2/3}\,dv\right),

and by Lemma 5,

∫01H2/3​(u)​𝑑u=Γ​(1/3)2Γ​(2/3).\int_{0}^{1}H_{2/3}(u)\,du=\frac{\Gamma(1/3)^{2}}{\Gamma(2/3)}.

Using ζ​(2)=π2/6\zeta(2)=\pi^{2}/6 and Γ​(1/3)​Γ​(2/3)=2​π/3\Gamma(1/3)\Gamma(2/3)=2\pi/\sqrt{3}, this simplifies to

Ress=2/3Zf​(s)=3​Γ​(1/3)322/3​π3​∫01|f′′​(v)|2/3​𝑑v.\operatorname*{Res}_{s=2/3}Z_{f}(s)=\frac{\sqrt{3}\,\Gamma(1/3)^{3}}{2^{2/3}\pi^{3}}\int_{0}^{1}|f^{\prime\prime}(v)|^{2/3}\,dv.

∎

Remark 13.

Theorem 9 strengthens the earlier disk-based local continuation statement. It is the analytic input used in Appendix B, subsection B.5.

Appendix D Zeta functions for convex domains: a survey

Several classical zeta functions may be attached to a convex domain, depending on which geometric profile one chooses to encode analytically. The main examples relevant here are:

  1. (1)

    gauge or lattice zeta functions, attached to lattice-point counting in dilates of the body;

  2. (2)

    distance and tube zeta functions, attached to the small-scale growth of Euclidean neighborhoods;

  3. (3)

    spectral zeta functions, attached to eigenvalue asymptotics of natural operators on the domain or on its boundary.

In each case, the zeta function is a Dirichlet series or Mellin transform whose first singularity is determined by the leading asymptotic term of the underlying counting or scale problem; see, for instance, [45, 40, 49]. The goal of this section is to recall these constructions in a form suited to comparison with the zeta function studied in the main text.

D.1. Gauge zetas, Hlawka zeta, and lattice-point asymptotics

Let K⊂ℝnK\subset\mathbb{R}^{n} be a convex body with 0∈int⁡K0\in\operatorname{int}K, and let

‖x‖K:=inf{t>0:x∈t​K}\|x\|_{K}:=\inf\{t>0:x\in tK\}

be its Minkowski functional. The associated gauge zeta function is

ZK​(s):=∑m∈ℤn∖{0}‖m‖K−s,ℜ⁡(s)>n.Z_{K}(s):=\sum_{m\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}\setminus\{0\}}\|m\|_{K}^{-s},\qquad\Re(s)>n.

Its natural counting function is

NK​(X):=#​{m∈ℤn:‖m‖K≤X}=#​(X​K∩ℤn).N_{K}(X):=\#\{m\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}:\|m\|_{K}\leq X\}=\#(XK\cap\mathbb{Z}^{n}).

Thus ZK​(s)Z_{K}(s) is the Mellin–Dirichlet transform naturally associated with lattice-point counting in dilates of KK. In particular, its first singularity is expected at the dimension s=ns=n, in accordance with the leading term vol⁡(K)​Xn\operatorname{vol}(K)X^{n} in lattice-point asymptotics [37, 45].

Restricting to primitive lattice points gives

ZKprim​(s):=∑m∈ℤn∖{0}gcd⁡(m1,…,mn)=1‖m‖K−s,ℜ⁡(s)>n,Z_{K}^{\mathrm{prim}}(s):=\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}m\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}\setminus\{0\}\\ \gcd(m_{1},\dots,m_{n})=1\end{subarray}}\|m\|_{K}^{-s},\qquad\Re(s)>n,

and homogeneity implies

ZK​(s)=ζ​(s)​ZKprim​(s)Z_{K}(s)=\zeta(s)\,Z_{K}^{\mathrm{prim}}(s)

in the common half-plane of absolute convergence.

In dimension 22, for a star-shaped domain D⊂ℝ2D\subset\mathbb{R}^{2} about the origin, one often writes t​(m,n)t(m,n) for the least t>0t>0 such that (m,n)∈t​D(m,n)\in tD, and defines the Hlawka zeta by

ZD​(s):=∑(m,n)≠(0,0)t​(m,n)−2​s,ℜ⁡(s)>1.Z_{D}(s):=\sum_{(m,n)\neq(0,0)}t(m,n)^{-2s},\qquad\Re(s)>1.

With this normalization, the natural pole is at s=1s=1, corresponding to the planar lattice-counting problem

AD​(x):=#​(x​D∩ℤ2),A_{D}(x):=\#(xD\cap\mathbb{Z}^{2}),

and this is the form in which the construction is usually discussed in the modern literature [45].

For the Euclidean disk one recovers

Zdisk​(s)=∑(m,n)≠(0,0)(m2+n2)−s,Z_{\mathrm{disk}}(s)=\sum_{(m,n)\neq(0,0)}(m^{2}+n^{2})^{-s},

the Epstein zeta function of the quadratic form x2+y2x^{2}+y^{2}. More generally, positive-definite binary quadratic forms lead to Epstein zeta functions, for which analytic continuation and functional equations are classical [13]. Primitive restrictions and boundary effects in convex lattice counting lead to finer arithmetic refinements [38].

D.2. Distance zetas, tube zetas, and Euclidean boundary geometry

Let A⊂ℝNA\subset\mathbb{R}^{N} be bounded and fix δ>0\delta>0. The distance zeta function of AA is

ζA(s):=∫Aδdist(x,A)s−Ndx,\zeta_{A}(s):=\int_{A_{\delta}}\operatorname{dist}(x,A)^{\,s-N}\,dx,

initially defined for ℜ⁡(s)\Re(s) sufficiently large. A basic theorem states that the abscissa of absolute convergence of ζA\zeta_{A} is the upper box dimension dim¯B​A\overline{\dim}_{B}A [39, 40].

A closely related object is the tube zeta function

ζ~A​(s):=∫0δts−N−1​|At|​𝑑t,\widetilde{\zeta}_{A}(s):=\int_{0}^{\delta}t^{\,s-N-1}|A_{t}|\,dt,

where At={x:dist⁡(x,A)<t}A_{t}=\{x:\operatorname{dist}(x,A)<t\}. The two zeta functions are linked by an explicit identity, and under suitable meromorphic continuation hypotheses their poles encode the small-tt asymptotics of |At||A_{t}| [39, 40].

For convex geometry the most natural choice is A=∂ΩA=\partial\Omega, where Ω⊂ℝN\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}^{N} is a bounded convex domain. If ∂Ω\partial\Omega is sufficiently regular (for example C1,1C^{1,1}, or more generally of positive reach), then the tube expansion is classical, and the first singularity occurs at s=N−1s=N-1, the dimension of the boundary. Under the usual Minkowski measurability hypotheses, the residue at this first pole is proportional to ℋN−1​(∂Ω)\mathcal{H}^{N-1}(\partial\Omega). For smooth convex bodies, the higher coefficients are the classical curvature coefficients appearing in the Steiner–Federer theory [14, 40].

D.3. Spectral zetas of the interior and of the boundary

Let Ω⊂ℝN\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}^{N} be a bounded domain with smooth boundary, and let

0<λ1≤λ2≤⋯→∞0<\lambda_{1}\leq\lambda_{2}\leq\cdots\to\infty

be the Dirichlet eigenvalues of the Laplacian. The spectral zeta function is

ζΔ​(s):=∑j=1∞λj−s,ℜ⁡(s)>N2.\zeta_{\Delta}(s):=\sum_{j=1}^{\infty}\lambda_{j}^{-s},\qquad\Re(s)>\frac{N}{2}.

Its meromorphic continuation is governed by the heat trace expansion

Tr⁡(e−t​Δ)∼∑j≥0cj​t(j−N)/2,t→0+,\operatorname{Tr}(e^{-t\Delta})\sim\sum_{j\geq 0}c_{j}\,t^{(j-N)/2},\qquad t\to 0+,

and therefore ζΔ​(s)\zeta_{\Delta}(s) has at most simple poles at

s=N−j2,j=0,1,2,…s=\frac{N-j}{2},\qquad j=0,1,2,\dots

[44, 49]. The leading pole at s=N/2s=N/2 is proportional to |Ω||\Omega|, while the next coefficient involves ℋN−1​(∂Ω)\mathcal{H}^{N-1}(\partial\Omega) [44, 49].

A boundary spectral analogue is provided by the Dirichlet-to-Neumann operator ΛΩ\Lambda_{\Omega}. In dimension 22, if Ω\Omega is a smooth simply connected planar domain and

0=σ0<σ1≤σ2≤⋯0=\sigma_{0}<\sigma_{1}\leq\sigma_{2}\leq\cdots

are the Steklov eigenvalues, one defines

ζSt​(s):=∑j=1∞σj−s.\zeta_{\mathrm{St}}(s):=\sum_{j=1}^{\infty}\sigma_{j}^{-s}.

Here the leading asymptotic is already one-dimensional: the Weyl law is governed by the boundary length L​(∂Ω)L(\partial\Omega), and correspondingly the principal singular behavior of ζSt​(s)\zeta_{\mathrm{St}}(s) is that of

2​(L​(∂Ω)2​π)s​ζR​(s)2\Bigl(\frac{L(\partial\Omega)}{2\pi}\Bigr)^{s}\zeta_{R}(s)

[16, 25, 26]. Thus the Dirichlet Laplacian and the Steklov problem produce different zeta functions on the same domain, reflecting, respectively, an interior asymptotic law and a boundary asymptotic law.

D.4. Comparison with the tropical zeta function of the present paper

We now place the tropical zeta function studied in this paper alongside the preceding constructions.

Let Ω⊂ℝ2\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}^{2} be a compact convex domain. The zeta function introduced in the main text is

ZΩ​(s)=∫ΩρΩ​(x)s−2​𝑑x,Z_{\Omega}(s)=\int_{\Omega}\rho_{\Omega}(x)^{\,s-2}\,dx,

where

ρΩ​(x)=minu∈ℤprim2⁡(⟨u,x⟩−hΩ​(u))\rho_{\Omega}(x)=\min_{u\in\mathbb{Z}^{2}_{\mathrm{prim}}}\bigl(\langle u,x\rangle-h_{\Omega}(u)\bigr)

is the tropical distance-to-the-boundary function defined by primitive lattice supporting lines. By Proposition 1, this admits the Mellin representation

ZΩ​(s)=∫0mΩts−2​PΩ​(t)​𝑑t,Z_{\Omega}(s)=\int_{0}^{m_{\Omega}}t^{s-2}P_{\Omega}(t)\,dt,

where PΩ​(t)P_{\Omega}(t) is the lattice perimeter of the tropical wave front

Ωt={x∈Ω:ρΩ​(x)≥t}.\Omega_{t}=\{x\in\Omega:\rho_{\Omega}(x)\geq t\}.

Thus, like distance, tube, and spectral zeta functions, the tropical zeta function is a Mellin transform of a geometric profile. The essential difference lies in the profile being Mellinized: here it is not Euclidean neighborhood growth, nor the spectrum of a differential operator, but the lattice perimeter of the wave fronts generated by primitive lattice support data.

This places the tropical zeta function closest in spirit to the gauge and Hlawka zeta functions, since all of them are built from the interaction of the convex body with the ambient lattice. However, the role of the lattice is different. In the gauge zeta

ZK​(s)=∑m∈ℤn∖{0}‖m‖K−s,Z_{K}(s)=\sum_{m\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}\setminus\{0\}}\|m\|_{K}^{-s},

the lattice enters through the distribution of lattice points in dilates of KK, so the first pole is governed by the main term in lattice-point counting, namely volume [37, 45]. By contrast, in the tropical zeta function the lattice enters through the primitive supporting directions of the boundary. The resulting zeta is still SL⁡(2,ℤ)\operatorname{SL}(2,\mathbb{Z})-invariant, but its singularities are governed by the boundary evolution of the tropical wave fronts rather than by the counting of lattice points inside dilates111111We will elaborate on the relation between these two problems in future work. Note also that the Hlawka zeta function is sensitive to translations of the domain, even by integer vectors, whereas the tropical zeta function is invariant under all real translations. This difference is in fact responsible for an obstruction to expressing the lattice-point error term solely in terms of the residues of the tropical zeta function..

This difference is already visible in the polygonal case. For rational polygons, the first pole of the tropical zeta function occurs at

s=1,s=1,

and its residue is the lattice perimeter of ∂Ω\partial\Omega; see Theorem 1 together with the computations of minimal models in Appendix A. Thus the leading singularity records a boundary quantity, not the area term coming from the interior. In this respect, the tropical zeta function is more boundary-sensitive than the gauge zeta and more arithmetic than the Euclidean distance and tube zetas.

Compared with distance and tube zeta functions, the contrast is even sharper. For

ζA(s)=∫Aδdist(x,A)s−Ndx\zeta_{A}(s)=\int_{A_{\delta}}\operatorname{dist}(x,A)^{\,s-N}\,dx

and

ζ~A​(s)=∫0δts−N−1​|At|​𝑑t,\widetilde{\zeta}_{A}(s)=\int_{0}^{\delta}t^{s-N-1}|A_{t}|\,dt,

the relevant small-scale profile is Euclidean, and for smooth convex boundaries the first pole is at the boundary dimension N−1N-1, with residue proportional to Euclidean boundary measure [39, 40, 14]. In the tropical case, one again has a Mellin transform of a boundary profile, but the profile is measured in the geometry of primitive lattice support planes. As a consequence, the first nontrivial pole in the smooth convex planar case with everywhere non-vanishing curvature is not at s=1s=1 but at

s=23,s=\frac{2}{3},

and the residue is proportional to equiaffine arc length:

Ress=2/3ZΩ​(s)=35/225/3​π3​Γ​(13)3​Lengthequiaffine⁡(∂Ω).\operatorname*{Res}_{s=2/3}Z_{\Omega}(s)=\frac{3^{5/2}}{2^{5/3}\pi^{3}}\Gamma\!\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{3}\operatorname{Length}_{\mathrm{equiaffine}}(\partial\Omega).

Thus, whereas Euclidean tube zeta functions recover Euclidean curvature data, the tropical zeta function recovers an affine boundary invariant.

The comparison with spectral zeta functions is also instructive. For the Dirichlet Laplacian, the poles of

ζΔ​(s)=∑j≥1λj−s\zeta_{\Delta}(s)=\sum_{j\geq 1}\lambda_{j}^{-s}

are governed by the heat trace, and the leading pole reflects the interior Weyl law, hence volume [44, 49]. For the Steklov zeta, the leading asymptotic is already boundary-based and determined by the boundary length [16, 25]. The tropical zeta function resembles the Steklov zeta in that its leading nontrivial asymptotics live on the boundary rather than in the interior. But it differs from both spectral examples in that it does not arise from the spectrum of an operator. Its analytic continuation is controlled instead by a boundary Dirichlet series over Farey neighbors and, ultimately, by incomplete Kloosterman-sum estimates and Legendre duality.

A second distinguishing feature of the tropical zeta function is its exact reduction to a boundary series. In dimension 22, Theorem 1 shows that

s​(s−1)​ZΩ​(s)=−F∂Ω​(s)+HΩ^​(s),s(s-1)Z_{\Omega}(s)=-F_{\partial\Omega}(s)+H_{\widehat{\Omega}}(s),

where HΩ^​(s)H_{\widehat{\Omega}}(s) is an explicit holomorphic correction term coming from the minimal model (see Proposition 6). Thus all nontrivial singular behavior is already encoded in the boundary Dirichlet series F∂Ω​(s)F_{\partial\Omega}(s). This kind of exact interior-to-boundary reduction does not occur in the same form for the classical gauge, tube, or spectral zeta functions recalled above.

Finally, from the arithmetic point of view, the tropical zeta function appears to be the construction among these examples that is most directly adapted to the study of rational and lattice points near convex curves. In the smooth convex case with everywhere non-vanishing curvature, its leading residue is equiaffine arc length, which is precisely the affine invariant that governs several quantitative problems on rational points and lattice points on convex arcs [46, 18, 19]. In this sense, the tropical zeta function is neither just Euclidean nor spectral: it is an SL⁡(2,ℤ)\operatorname{SL}(2,\mathbb{Z})-arithmetic zeta whose first genuinely smooth residue is SL⁡(2,ℝ)\operatorname{SL}(2,\mathbb{R})-affine.

To summarize, the tropical zeta function shares with the classical constructions the general Dirichlet–Mellin principle that poles encode asymptotic geometry, but it differs from them in three essential respects:

  1. (1)

    its defining geometric input is the tropical distance function built from primitive lattice support directions;

  2. (2)

    in dimension 22, its singularities are governed by an exact boundary Dirichlet series over Farey neighbors;

  3. (3)

    for smooth convex domains with everywhere non-vanishing curvature, its first nontrivial pole detects equiaffine arc length rather than Euclidean boundary measure, volume, or spectral Weyl data.

This combination of lattice symmetry, boundary reduction, and affine residue appears to be specific to the tropical zeta function introduced in this paper.

The tropical zeta function thus places the geometry of convex domains into a single analytic framework in which discrete lattice data and smooth affine geometry appear as different singular regimes of the same object. In the polygonal case, the first pole detects the lattice-visible boundary through the tropical perimeter; in the smooth strictly convex case, the leading nontrivial residue is the equiaffine perimeter. The mechanism behind this transition is the exact reduction of the interior Mellin integral to a boundary Dirichlet series, whose arithmetic structure is governed by Farey neighbors and whose local model is the primitive Mordell–Tornheim, equivalently Witten SU​(3)\mathrm{SU}(3), zeta function. From this perspective, the pole at s=23s=\tfrac{2}{3} is not an isolated accident but the first smooth affine manifestation of a tropical-lattice analytic theory of convexity. The broader continuation problem, the higher-dimensional case, and the relation to lattice-point error terms remain open, but the results obtained here already show that tropical optics provides a natural zeta-theoretic passage from arithmetic boundary data to equiaffine geometry.

Experimental support, please view the build logs for errors. Generated by L A T E xml .

Instructions for reporting errors

We are continuing to improve HTML versions of papers, and your feedback helps enhance accessibility and mobile support. To report errors in the HTML that will help us improve conversion and rendering, choose any of the methods listed below:

  • Click the "Report Issue" () button, located in the page header.

Tip: You can select the relevant text first, to include it in your report.

Our team has already identified the following issues. We appreciate your time reviewing and reporting rendering errors we may not have found yet. Your efforts will help us improve the HTML versions for all readers, because disability should not be a barrier to accessing research. Thank you for your continued support in championing open access for all.

Have a free development cycle? Help support accessibility at arXiv! Our collaborators at LaTeXML maintain a list of packages that need conversion, and welcome developer contributions.

BETA
